title: Environment Variables
description: Learn how to use variables for dynamic command customization

sections.title: What Are Variables?
sections.content:
Variables are named placeholders that store values you can use in aliases and triggers. They make your automation more flexible and reusable.

Instead of hardcoding values, you can use variables that are easy to change.

sections.title: Variable Syntax
sections.content:
Use the ${variable_name} syntax to reference variables:

| Variable | Usage | Example Result |
|----------|-------|----------------|
| target | cast ${target} | cast goblin |
| heal | drink ${heal} | drink potion |
| attack | kill ${attack} | kill dragon |

The ${...} is replaced with the variable's value when the command is sent.

sections.title: Creating a Variable
sections.content:
To create a variable:

1. Go to Connection Settings
2. Navigate to Environment Variables
3. Click 'Add Variable'
4. Enter a name (e.g., 'target')
5. Enter the value (e.g., 'goblin')
6. Click Save

Your variable is available immediately in aliases and triggers.

sections.title: Using Variables in Aliases
sections.content:
Variables work seamlessly with aliases:

Example 1 - Combat alias:
- Pattern: 'k'
- Type: prefix
- Replacement: kill ${target}

When you type 'k goblin', it sends 'kill goblin'.
If you change target='dragon', it sends 'kill dragon'.

Example 2 - Healer alias:
- Pattern: 'h'
- Type: exact
- Replacement: heal ${heal}

Type 'h' to drink your configured healing item.

sections.title: Using Variables in Triggers
sections.content:
Variables can also be used in trigger commands:

Example:
- Match: 'You are hungry'
- Action: 'eat ${food}'

When triggered, it sends 'eat bread' (or whatever food is set to).

sections.title: Variable Persistence
sections.content:
Variables are saved with your connection profile:

- They persist across sessions
- They're specific to each saved connection
- Changing a variable affects all aliases/triggers that use it

This makes it easy to switch between different targets or configurations.

sections.title: Example Workflow
sections.content:
Here's a practical example:

1. Create variable: target = goblin
2. Create variable: heal = potion
3. Create alias: pattern='k', replacement='kill ${target}'
4. Create alias: pattern='dr', replacement='drink ${heal}'

Now:
- Type 'k' to kill goblins
- Type 'dr' to drink potions

When you want to fight dragons instead:
- Change target = dragon
- Now 'k' kills dragons automatically!

Use the 'Add Example' button for ready-to-use variable templates.
